chunk_summary: Recurrent diarrhea in 6–24-month-olds can result from unsafe food storage practices. Clinic assessment emphasizes practical safety plans, including immediate feeding, safe water use, and hygiene reinforcement.
learning_objectives: Understand the link between unsafe food storage and recurrent diarrhea in infants aged 6–24 months.
Learn practical interventions for food safety in homes without refrigeration.
Recognize when to escalate evaluation for chronic or severe diarrhea in infants.
key_facts: Cooked foods kept at room temperature for more than ~2 hours increase microbial contamination risk.
Recurrent diarrhea in infants can occur every 2–3 weeks due to unsafe food storage and water practices.
Bottle and teat use is discouraged due to cleaning challenges; cup and spoon feeding is safer.
Boiled or treated water should be used for drinking and food preparation, and stored covered.
Handwashing with soap before food preparation and feeding reduces contamination risk.
reference_quote: Cooked infant foods are safest when fed immediately; if not refrigerated, discard foods kept at room temperature for more than ~2 hours and reheat until very hot throughout before feeding. (WHO Five Keys to Safer Food Manual)
document_key_topics: Complementary feeding safety for infants 6–24 months
Recurrent diarrhea prevention through food and water hygiene
Practical home-based interventions for food storage
